title: Ensure that UDP access from the Internet is evaluated and  restricted

input_row.title: Ensure that UDP access from the Internet is evaluated and  restricted
input_row.description: Network security groups should be periodically evaluated for port misconfigurations. Where UDP is not explicitly required and narrowly configured for resources attached to a network security group, Internet-level access to Azure resources should be restricted or eliminated.
input_row.rationale: The potential security problem with broadly exposing UDP services over the Internet is that attackers can use DDoS amplification techniques to reflect spoofed UDP traffic from Azure Virtual Machines. The most common types of these attacks exploit exposed DNS, NTP, SSDP, SNMP, CLDAP, and other UDP-based services as amplification sources to disrupt services on other machines within the Azure Virtual Network, or even attack networked devices outside of Azure.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Network security groups. 2. Click the name of a network security group. 3. Under Settings, click Inbound security rules. 4. Ensure that no inbound security rule exists that matches the following: o Port: 53, 123, 161, 389, or 1900, or range including 53, 123, 161, 389, or 1900, or other vulnerable UDP-based services o Protocol: UDP or Any o Source: 0.0.0.0/0, Internet, or Any o Action: Allow 5. Repeat steps 1-4 for each network security group. To audit from Azure Resource Graph: 1. Go to Resource Graph Explorer. 2. Click New query. 3. Paste the following into the query window: 4. resources | where type =~ "microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups" | project id, name, securityRule = properties.securityRules | mv-expand securityRule | extend access = securityRule.properties.access, direction = securityRule.properties.direction, protocol = securityRule.properties.protocol, destinationPort = case(isempty(securityRule.properties.destinationPortRange), securityRule.properties.destinationPortRanges, securityRule.properties.destinationPortRange), sourceAddress = case(isempty(securityRule.properties.sourceAddressPrefix), securityRule.properties.sourceAddressPrefixes, securityRule.properties.sourceAddressPrefix) | where access =~ "Allow" and direction =~ "Inbound" and protocol in~ ("udp", "*") | mv-expand destinationPort | mv-expand sourceAddress | extend destinationPortMin = toint(split(destinationPort, "-")[0]), destinationPortMax = toint(split(destinationPort, "-")[-1]) | where (destinationPortMin <= 53 and destinationPortMax >= 53) or (destinationPortMin <= 123 and destinationPortMax >= 123) or (destinationPortMin <= 161 and destinationPortMax >= 161) or (destinationPortMin <= 389 and destinationPortMax >= 389) or (destinationPortMin <= 1900 and destinationPortMax >= 1900) or destinationPort == "" | where sourceAddress in~ ("*", "0.0.0.0", "internet", "any") or sourceAddress endswith "/0" 5. Click Run query. 6. Ensure that no results are returned. Audit from Azure CLI List network security groups with non-default security rules: az network nsg list --query [*].[name,securityRules] Ensure that no network security group has an inbound security rule that matches the following: "access" : "Allow" "destinationPortRange" : "53", "123", "161", "389", "1900", "*" or "" "direction" : "Inbound" "protocol" : "UDP" or "*" "sourceAddressPrefix" : "0.0.0.0/0", "Internet", or "*" Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 9daedab3-fb2d-461e-b861-71790eead4f6 - Name: 'All network ports should be restricted on network security groups associated to your virtual machine'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Network security groups. 2. Click the name of a network security group. 3. Under Settings, click Inbound security rules. 4. Check the box next to any inbound security rule matching: o Port: Port: 53, 123, 161, 389, or 1900, or range including 53, 123, 161, 389, or 1900, or other vulnerable UDP-based services o Protocol: UDP or Any o Source: 0.0.0.0/0, Internet, or Any o Action: Allow 5. Click Delete. 6. Click Yes. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each network security group requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each network security group rule requiring remediation, run the following command to delete the rule: az network nsg rule delete --resource-group --nsg-name --name
input_row.references: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/network-best- practices#secure-your-critical-azure-service-resources-to-only-your-virtual- networks 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ddos-protection/fundamental-best- practices 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-network- security#ns-1-establish-network-segmentation-boundaries 4. ExpressRoute: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/expressroute/ 5. Site-to-site VPN: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/tutorial- site-to-site-portal 6. Point-to-site VPN: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/vpn-gateway/point-to- site-certificate-gateway
input_row.default_value: By default, UDP access from internet is not enabled.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_ec2_security_group_udp_access_restrictions_checked

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- Use AWS CLI commands or Boto3 SDK methods to check the security groups:
  - **Retrieve Security Groups**: Use `boto3.ec2.describe_security_groups()` or the CLI command `aws ec2 describe-security-groups` to list all security groups.
  - **Check Inbound Rules**: For each security group, evaluate the inbound rules for:
    - `IpProtocol` set to `udp` or `-1` (which means any protocol),
    - `FromPort` being in the set {53, 123, 161, 389, 1900} or any range including these ports,
    - `CidrIp` set to `0.0.0.0/0` (allowing access from any IP).
  
- **Pass/Fail Criteria**:
  - Pass: The security group does not have any inbound rules with `CidrIp` as `0.0.0.0/0` and ports 53, 123, 161, 389, or 1900 open to UDP.
  - Fail: At least one inbound rule exists that allows UDP traffic from `0.0.0.0/0` on the specified ports.

This approach leverages existing AWS APIs to automate the audit of security group configurations, ensuring compliance with best practices regarding UDP access controls.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task of evaluating security group configurations can be effectively automated using AWS APIs, specifically with boto3 or AWS CLI, which are well-suited for this type of repetitive and data-intensive task.
- AWS SDK capabilities, particularly boto3, provide the necessary methods (`describe_security_groups`) to retrieve and evaluate security group rules, making automation feasible and efficient.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of `boto3.ec2.describe_security_groups()` and the CLI command `aws ec2 describe-security-groups` is appropriate for retrieving security group information.
- The criteria for checking inbound rules are generally accurate, focusing on `IpProtocol` set to `udp` or `-1`, specific ports, and `CidrIp` set to `0.0.0.0/0`.
- The program name `aws_ec2_security_group_udp_access_restrictions_checked` is descriptive but could be clearer. It doesn't specify the exact nature of the check, such as the focus on specific UDP ports and public access.
- The approach does not consider IPv6 (`::/0`), which should also be evaluated for completeness.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **IPv6 Consideration:** Include checks for `CidrIpv6` set to `::/0` to ensure that IPv6 traffic is also evaluated.
- **Program Name:** A more descriptive name could be `aws_ec2_security_group_udp_public_access_check`. This name better reflects the focus on public access and specific UDP ports.
- **Additional Ports:** Consider documenting why only these specific ports are checked. If other UDP ports are relevant, they should be included or justified for exclusion.
- **Logging and Reporting:** Implement logging for audit trails and generate a report summarizing findings for compliance documentation.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded on AWS capabilities, and the automation approach is appropriate for the task. The technical details are mostly accurate, with minor improvements suggested.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify** the automation script to include checks for IPv6 (`CidrIpv6` set to `::/0`).
- **Modify** the program name to `aws_ec2_security_group_udp_public_access_check` for clarity and specificity.
- Ensure documentation includes rationale for port selection and any additional security considerations.
